---
title: "Test-mirai"
format: html
editor: visual
---

## Quarto

Quarto enables you to weave together content and executable code into a finished document. To learn more about Quarto see <https://quarto.org>.

## Running Code

When you click the **Render** button a document will be generated that includes both content and the output of embedded code. You can embed code like this:

```{r}
1 + 1
```

You can add options to executable code like this

```{r}
#| echo: false
2 * 2
```

The `echo: false` option disables the printing of code (only output is displayed).

## mirai

[mirai](https://mirai.r-lib.org) is a package for developing asynchronous code
so that you can write code that runs in the background while you do other things. To learn more about mirai see <https://mirai.r-lib.org>.

## First example

```{r}
library(mirai)
# create an mirai object
m <- mirai({
  Sys.sleep(5)
  "Hello, world!"
})
```

The code above creates an mirai object that will run the code inside the curly braces in the background (in a different R session). The code will sleep for 5 seconds and then return "Hello, world!".

To get the result of the mirai object, one can do the following:

```{r}
m$data
```


You can also collect the results using bracket operator:

```{r}
m[] # block until it its complete
```


If a mirai object has not been completed, then running the following command will
show `TRUE`:

```{r}
unresolved(m)
```

## mirai_map()

The `mirai_map()` function allows you to run a function on multiple inputs in parallel. For example, the following code will run the `Sys.sleep()` function on the numbers 1 to 5 in parallel, but before that you need set up a cluster using the function
`mirai::daemons()`:

```{r}
mirai::daemons(2) # set up a cluster with 2 workers
mirai_map(1:5, function(x) {
  Sys.sleep(x)
  x
})
```

One can use `info()` to check the status of a the mirai cluster.

## No inheritence of global variables

When one defines a mirai object, the code inside the curly braces will not inherit any global variables. For example, the following code will return an error because `x` is not defined inside the mirai object:

```{r, eval=FALSE}
x <- 10
m2 <- mirai({
  x + 1
})
m2$data
```


## Setup daemons in a dynamic way

One can use `local_url()` to setup local connections. For example,
we will start a local cluster of 2 workers first, and then add another
3 workers later

```{r}
daemons(url = local_url())
launch_local(2) # launch 2 workers
Sys.sleep(1) # sleep for a bit to leter the cluster to spin up
info() # check the status of the cluster
```

Later, we can add another 3 more:

```{r}
launch_local(3) # launch 3 more workers
Sys.sleep(1)
info() # check the status of the cluster
```


Additionally, one can create multiple clusters by providing the parameter
`.compute` to `deamons()`. Here are two examples:

```{r, eval=FALSE}
daemons(2, .compute = "cluster1")
daemons(3, .compute = "cluster2")
# now use the two clusters in parallel
mirai(Sys.sleep(1), .compute = "cluster1")
mirai(Sys.sleep(1), .compute = "cluster2")
```


To close the clusters, run the following command:

```{r}
daemons(0)
info()
```

## Connect daemons on a remote machine

To do this, one can create daemons on a remote machine using the following
ssh settings:

```{r, eval=FALSE}
daemons(
  n = 4,
  url = local_url(tcp = TRUE),
  remote = ssh_config("ssh://user@node1", tunnel = TRUE)
)
```

This will create 4 workers on the remote machine `node1` and connect them to the local machine using SSH tunneling. The `tunnel = TRUE` option will automatically set up SSH tunneling for the connection. Check the function `ssh_config()` for more details on how to set up the SSH connection, including the path to `Rscript` in the remote machine.

Note that for this to work, one need to make sure the listened ports in remote and local
machine are all reachable, not blocked by tools such as Zscaler.
